---
title: "ML Math — Mathematics for Machine Learning"
subtitle: "Deisenroth, Faisal & Ong's *Mathematics for Machine Learning* (Cambridge 2024), lesson by lesson — every classical ML algorithm derived from its underlying maths."
---

## Why this track exists

The seven synthesis tiers cover the **mathematics** machine learning
needs — linear algebra (T5), eigenvalues and SVD (T5–T6), partial
derivatives and gradients (T5), probability and the Gaussian (T5),
convex optimisation and gradient methods (T6). What they don't do is
walk through the classical ML algorithms themselves: linear regression
as MLE *and* as a Bayesian posterior, PCA from four perspectives, GMM
fitted by EM, SVMs with kernels. Those algorithms — the *first*
machine-learning algorithms anyone learns — sit in a no-man's-land
between the maths and the deep-learning frameworks.

This track is the **bridge**. It takes one carefully-chosen reference
(Deisenroth, Faisal & Ong's *Mathematics for Machine Learning*,
Cambridge 2024) and walks lesson by lesson from "solve $Ax = b$" to
"the kernel trick." Every algorithm is *derived* — not recited. By
the end, the "classical ML zoo" feels like five applications of the
same handful of decomposition / probability / optimisation moves,
because that's what it is.

## What this track is for

Use this track if you want:

- **The classical ML curriculum, derived.** Linear regression, PCA,
  GMM with EM, and SVM with kernels — each one built from the maths,
  not from a `scikit-learn` call.
- **Backpropagation, properly.** Most resources teach gradient
  descent and skip the mechanics; this track derives autodiff and
  backprop as repeated chain-rule on a computation graph.
- **Probability that lands.** The Gaussian, conjugate priors, the
  exponential family, and change-of-variables — treated as four
  building blocks for Bayesian models, not as four disconnected facts.
- **The four-perspective view of PCA.** Max-variance, projection-error,
  eigenvector computation, latent-variable — each perspective is a
  separate lesson, and the connections are explicit.
- **Strict prerequisite discipline.** Each lesson depends on the
  previous one *and* declares its prerequisites in the synthesis
  tiers. Arrive without matrix algebra? You'll be sent to T5.01.
  Arrive without partial derivatives? T5.05.

## Source

[**Deisenroth, Faisal & Ong** — *Mathematics for Machine Learning*, Cambridge 2024](https://mml-book.com)

The free PDF at `mml-book.com` is the canonical reference. The book
covers the same five algorithms (Linear Regression, PCA, GMM, SVM,
plus a unifying "When Models Meet Data" chapter) and the seven
foundational maths chapters this track follows in order.

::: {.callout-note}
**Citation, not transcription.** Per the same rule that governs the
Kiselev and MFRL tracks, this track cites Deisenroth/Faisal/Ong freely
(chapter and section numbers, theorem statements, structural choices,
notation) but does *not* paste the book's prose. Worked examples are
re-stated in our own words. The four-perspective PCA framing is the
authors' pedagogical structure — that idea belongs to them and is cited
in every PCA lesson.
:::

## How an `mml` lesson is structured

The **same six-beat contract** as every other lesson in this repo
(*Touch* → *Wonder* → *Discover* → *Name* → *Apply* → *Connect*),
plus the four required callouts every lesson carries:

- One `.fun-fact` (history / origin hook)
- One `.key-insight` (the intuitive aha)
- A `### Takeaways` block (3 bullets, intuitive)
- Inline glossary cross-links

Unlike CBSE and Kiselev, this track has no per-lesson tier-specific
addition (no "Board-Pattern Practice" or "Kiselev-style problems"
block). The recurring visual anchor — the *design matrix*
$\boldsymbol{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D}$ — appears in *Touch*
widgets across many lessons, not as a structural beat.

See [CLAUDE.md](https://github.com/rajeshpillai/applied-maths/blob/main/CLAUDE.md)
for the full authoring contract.

## Track structure

The track follows the book's two-part division:

### Part I — Mathematical Foundations (lessons 1-21)

The maths every classical ML algorithm rests on: linear algebra,
analytic geometry, matrix decompositions, vector calculus,
probability, continuous optimisation. These chapters earn the right
to *all later algorithms* — they are not optional.

| # | Topic | Chapter |
|---|---|---|
| 1-6 | Linear algebra: systems, matrices, vector spaces, basis, rank, affine spaces | Ch 2 |
| 7-9 | Analytic geometry: norms, inner products, projections, rotations | Ch 3 |
| 10-13 | Matrix decompositions: determinant, Cholesky, eigendecomposition, SVD | Ch 4 |
| 14-16 | Vector calculus: gradients, vector/matrix calculus, backpropagation | Ch 5 |
| 17-19 | Probability: spaces, Gaussian, conjugacy, change-of-variables | Ch 6 |
| 20-21 | Optimisation: gradient descent, constrained / convex (Lagrange, KKT) | Ch 7 |

### Part II — Central Machine-Learning Problems (lessons 22-32)

Where the foundations cash out into the algorithms that built classical
machine learning: ERM, linear regression, PCA, GMM with EM, SVM with
kernels.

| # | Topic | Chapter |
|---|---|---|
| 22-23 | When models meet data: ERM, parameter estimation, graphical models, model selection | Ch 8 |
| 24-25 | Linear regression: MLE, Bayesian LR, MLE as orthogonal projection | Ch 9 |
| 26-28 | PCA: max-variance, projection-error, eigenvector, latent-variable | Ch 10 |
| 29-30 | Gaussian Mixture Models and the EM algorithm | Ch 11 |
| 31-32 | SVM: primal, dual, kernels | Ch 12 |

## Prerequisites from the synthesis curriculum

Every `mml` lesson declares its math prerequisites by linking back
into the T-tiers. The complete list:

| Prerequisite | What it gives us |
|---|---|
| [`t5.linear-systems-gaussian`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/02-linear-systems-gaussian/index.qmd) | Gaussian elimination, solution geometry |
| [`t5.matrices`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/01-matrices/index.qmd) | Matrix algebra, inverses |
| [`t5.eigenvalues`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd) | Eigenvalues / eigenvectors for decompositions |
| [`t5.partial-derivatives`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd) | Gradients, vector calculus |
| [`t5.continuous-distributions`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd) | PDFs, integrals as expectations |
| [`t5.variance-clt`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd) | Variance, covariance, CLT |
| [`t5.statistical-inference`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/10-statistical-inference/index.qmd) | MLE, Bayes, posteriors |
| [`t6.spectral-theorem`](../lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/04-spectral-theorem/index.qmd) | Spectral view of SVD |
| [`t6.gradient-methods`](../lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/10-gradient-methods/index.qmd) | SGD, momentum, learning rate schedules |
| [`t6.convex-optimization`](../lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/03-convex-optimization/index.qmd) | Convex sets, convex functions, duality |

If any of these prerequisites is missing or under-sized for the new
track, that's a hint to add coverage in the synthesis tier rather
than duplicate inside `mml`.

## How to read this track

- **Read in order.** Each lesson depends on the previous one by
  design. Skipping forward will leave gaps that the prerequisite
  graph cannot patch.
- **Compare with the synthesis tiers** when you want the maths
  without ML framing — Tier 5–6 lessons give you the pure
  linear-algebra / calculus / probability view.
- **Read alongside the MML PDF** (free at `mml-book.com`). This
  track adds the *Touch* widget, the *Wonder* questions, the
  graduated hints, and the prerequisite scaffolding the book's
  graduate-textbook compactness leaves to the reader.

## Track status — in progress

The track is being authored against the six-beat contract. See
the [tier index](../lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/index.qmd) for the
per-lesson list and current `done` / `planned` status.
